criteria.label: 1. Explanatory scope
criteria.desc: Explains more of the accepted facts than rivals.

criteria.label: 2. Explanatory power
criteria.desc: Makes the facts more probable than rivals do.

criteria.label: 3. Plausibility
criteria.desc: Fits better with background knowledge than rivals.

criteria.label: 4. Ad hoc assumptions
criteria.desc: Requires fewer new/unsupported assumptions than rivals.

criteria.label: 5. Disconfirming beliefs
criteria.desc: Is disconfirmed by fewer accepted beliefs than rivals.

criteria.label: 6. Overall vs. rivals (best explanation)
criteria.desc: Significantly exceeds rivals overall on these criteria.

scoreNotes.summary: No change: scores already reflect Craig's strong endorsement (high scope/power/exceeds) and worldview-dependent plausibility.
scoreNotes.plausibility: Kept below perfect (0.76) to reflect Craig's qualification that plausibility depends on prior theistic commitment.

notes.scope: It accounts for a wide range of agreed-upon facts — from the burial by Joseph of Arimathea and the empty tomb discovered by women, to the postmortem appearances and the conversions of skeptics like Paul and James. The sudden rise of the Christian movement in Jerusalem, where verification would have been possible, further widens its explanatory scope.
notes.power: The hypothesis has strong predictive power: if Jesus rose, the presence of appearances, transformed lives, and enduring proclamation follow naturally. By contrast, rival hypotheses leave much of the evidence unaccounted for or render it improbable.
notes.plausibility: Its plausibility depends largely on whether one is open to divine action. Within a theistic worldview, a resurrection fits coherently as God’s vindication of Jesus’ radical claims. Even in a neutral first-century Jewish context, resurrection belief was not alien but aligned with eschatological hopes.
notes.adHoc: It requires only one auxiliary assumption — that God exists and can act. No contrived secondary stories are needed to patch holes; the hypothesis is parsimonious in its commitments.
notes.notDisconfirmed: It does not conflict with accepted background beliefs. The objection that people do not naturally rise from the dead is consistent with the claim, since the hypothesis posits supernatural causation rather than natural anomaly.
notes.exceeds: When weighed against competitors, it consistently fares better across all seven criteria, giving it a cumulative superiority as the best explanation of the historical facts.

plausibility.t: It is plausible: given the historical context of Jesus’s own unparalleled life and claims, the resurrection serves as divine confirmation of those radical claims.

adHoc.t: It is not ad hoc or contrived; it requires only one additional hypothesis: that God exists.

notDisconfirmed.t: It is in accord with accepted beliefs. The hypothesis: 'God raised Jesus from the dead' does not in any way conflict with the accepted belief that people don’t rise naturally from the dead.

exceeds.t: The Resurrection hypothesis passes all of the standard criteria for being the best explanation.

scoreNotes.summary: Kept several criteria low to reflect the Defenders 3 Part 44 case that hallucinations cannot explain the empty tomb or the origin of the disciples' belief, and fit the appearance data only weakly.
scoreNotes.scope: Craig treats scope as narrow for hallucination; lowered to 0.25 to reflect inability to explain the empty tomb or mass appearances.
scoreNotes.power: Lowered to 0.30: poor fit for consistent multi-person/group appearances and conversions.
scoreNotes.plausibility: Lowered to 0.30 for psychological implausibility when applied to the full corpus of appearances.
scoreNotes.adHoc: Lowered slightly: requires auxiliary hypotheses (e.g., theft) to cover tomb evidence.
scoreNotes.exceeds: Kept low because Craig's scholarly assessment is that the hallucination hypothesis has not demonstrated clear superiority to rival theories, including the resurrection hypothesis.

notes.scope: The hallucination hypothesis has narrow scope because it only attempts to explain the postmortem appearances. It says nothing to explain the empty tomb and, in a Jewish context, it also fails to explain why the disciples concluded resurrection rather than assumption into heaven or a vision of a deceased person.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is weak even for the appearances themselves. The appearance traditions are too diverse in number, setting, recipients, and group character, and they include skeptics like James and enemies like Saul, so the theory has to cobble together a composite picture from disparate psychological cases.
notes.plausibility: The theory is implausible on two fronts. Ludemann's psychoanalysis of Peter and Paul rests on disputed depth psychology and too little historical data, and the further claim that all the appearances were merely visionary wrongly treats Paul's Damascus experience as the model for every other appearance.
notes.adHoc: Ludemann's version is ad hoc in several ways: it sends the disciples back to Galilee so they cannot check the tomb, assumes a chain reaction of hallucinations among the disciples, and attributes to Paul a subconscious attraction to Christianity without evidence.
notes.notDisconfirmed: The theory is disconfirmed by accepted beliefs among New Testament scholars: Jesus received honorable burial from Joseph of Arimathea, the tomb was found empty by women followers, psychoanalysis of historical figures is not feasible, Paul was not crushed by guilt under the law, and the New Testament distinguishes visions from resurrection appearances.
notes.exceeds: The hallucination hypothesis does not clearly surpass rival theories, including the resurrection hypothesis. It remains a discussed option, but it does not emerge as the strongest competing explanation overall.

scope.t: The hallucination hypothesis obviously suffers from narrow explanatory scope.

power.t: there is nothing like the resurrection appearances in the psychological case books.

plausibility.t: Thus the hallucination hypothesis is implausible because of its tendency to try to reduce all of the postmortem appearances to mere visions.

adHoc.t: Ludemann's version of the hallucination hypothesis is ad hoc in a number of ways.

notDisconfirmed.t: Ludemann's hypothesis, being inconsistent with all of these, tends to be disconfirmed by accepted beliefs among New Testament Scholars.

exceeds.t: the Hallucination Hypothesis has not demonstrated its clear superiority to rival theories, including the Resurrection Hypothesis.

scoreNotes.summary: Adjusted to reflect Craig's view: conspiracy is granted adequate scope in the bare sense that it can be framed to address the three main facts, but it is still implausible and highly ad hoc.
scoreNotes.scope: Kept at 0.60 because Craig explicitly grants the conspiracy hypothesis adequate explanatory scope by letting it cover the empty tomb, appearances, and origin of belief.
scoreNotes.power: Lowered to 0.25: fails to render the disciples' transformation and martyrdom probable.
scoreNotes.plausibility: Lowered to 0.20: Craig emphasizes psychological and historical implausibility.
scoreNotes.adHoc: Lowered to 0.22 to reflect many unsupported auxiliary claims required.
scoreNotes.notDisconfirmed: Lowered to 0.25 reflecting conflicts with general knowledge about conspiracies and disciple sincerity.

notes.scope: The conspiracy hypothesis has adequate scope in a bare formal sense: it can be made to cover the empty tomb by theft, the appearances by lying, and the disciples' belief by fraud. Its problem is not coverage, but whether those explanations actually fit the evidence.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is weak. The empty-tomb story contains features a hoaxer would likely avoid, fabricated appearances should have looked more like glorious scriptural visions, and the disciples' transformed conviction is not well explained if they were deliberate deceivers.
notes.plausibility: The theory's Achilles heel is its implausibility, especially its anachronism. First-century Jews did not expect a crucified Messiah or an isolated resurrection before the end of the age, so stealing the body and proclaiming resurrection is not the kind of scheme these disciples would naturally invent.
notes.adHoc: The theory is highly ad hoc because it explains away the evidence while assigning motives, ideas, and actions to the disciples for which there is no independent support. It then needs more patches to handle details like the women at the tomb and the report of the five hundred.
notes.notDisconfirmed: It is disconfirmed by accepted beliefs about how conspiracies tend to unravel, by the widely accepted sincerity of the disciples, and by what is known about first-century Jewish messianic expectations.
notes.exceeds: It does not surpass rival hypotheses on the historical criteria; even hallucination is more plausible because it does not have to reduce the disciples' belief to conscious fraud. That helps explain why modern scholarship has abandoned the conspiracy hypothesis.

scope.t: The conspiracy hypothesis does seem to have adequate explanatory scope.

power.t: The transformation in the lives of these disciples is not plausibly explained by the hypothesis of a conspiracy.

plausibility.t: The real Achilles heel of the conspiracy hypothesis is, I think, its implausibility.

adHoc.t: You just have to keep accumulating ad hoc hypotheses in order to explain the data.

notDisconfirmed.t: The conspiracy hypothesis tends to be disconfirmed by our general knowledge of conspiracies.

exceeds.t: The bottom line is that no scholar today would accept the conspiracy hypothesis.

scoreNotes.summary: Lowered to reflect Craig's dismissal: wrong-tomb explains at best a mistaken empty-tomb report and fails for appearances and origin of belief.
scoreNotes.power: Kept low (0.09): no real power regarding appearances or conversions.

notes.scope: The wrong-tomb hypothesis does not have sufficiently wide scope because it says nothing about the postmortem appearances and therefore needs an additional hypothesis to cover the full evidence. At best it tries to explain the empty tomb report, while leaving the appearances and the disciples' resurrection belief substantially untouched.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is minimal. It has zero power regarding the appearances, does not plausibly explain how a visit to the wrong tomb would lead first-century Jews to believe Jesus had been raised, and would have been exposed once the disciples or their opponents checked the actual burial site.
notes.plausibility: The theory is implausible because the tomb location was known in Jerusalem to both Jews and Christians, the empty-tomb tradition is extremely early and undeveloped, and any attempt to supplement the theory with hallucinations only imports further implausibility.
notes.adHoc: The theory is ad hoc because it handles the evidence selectively. It accepts the women's visit and their intention to anoint the body while ignoring that this same intention explains why they had noted the tomb's location, and it arbitrarily treats some elements of Mark's account as historical while discarding others.
notes.notDisconfirmed: It is disconfirmed by accepted beliefs that Joseph of Arimathea buried Jesus and could identify the tomb. To avoid that problem, the theory must invent further unsupported assumptions, such as Joseph and any helpers becoming unavailable before they could correct the mistake.
notes.exceeds: The wrong-tomb hypothesis does not surpass its rivals on the historical criteria and is not regarded as a serious competitor among scholars.

scope.t: In that sense the theory does not have sufficiently wide explanatory scope.

power.t: It obviously has zero explanatory power when it comes to the postmortem appearances.

plausibility.t: The wrong tomb hypothesis is also implausible in light of the evidence that we have that the site of Jesus' tomb was known to Jew and Christian alike in Jerusalem.

adHoc.t: Lake's theory is ad hoc in that it treats the evidence selectively and arbitrarily.

notDisconfirmed.t: The wrong tomb hypothesis is disconfirmed by the generally accepted beliefs that Joseph of Arimathea did bury Jesus in the tomb and thus could point to the correct burial location.

exceeds.t: Again, nobody thinks that the wrong tomb hypothesis outstrips its rival hypotheses.

scoreNotes.summary: Scored low overall: displaced-body explanations are narrow and implausible, but Craig treats their ad hocness as only mild rather than extreme.
scoreNotes.adHoc: Raised to 0.46 because Craig says the theory is not greatly ad hoc, but only somewhat ad hoc.

notes.scope: The displaced-body hypothesis has narrow scope because it only tries to explain the empty tomb. It says nothing about the postmortem appearances or the origin of the disciples' resurrection belief, so additional hypotheses are required to cover the full evidence.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is weak even on its own terms. It has no power regarding the appearances or the disciples' belief, and it also fails to explain why Joseph of Arimathea or anyone who helped bury Jesus did not correct the disciples if the body had merely been moved.
notes.plausibility: The theory is implausible because Jewish burial practice already provided a nearby criminals' graveyard, Joseph as a Sanhedrist would have wanted to bury an executed criminal properly the same day, and Jewish law did not permit moving the corpse later in the way the hypothesis requires.
notes.adHoc: The theory is only mildly ad hoc, not wildly contrived. Still, it assigns Joseph motives and actions for which there is no independent evidence, so the explanation depends on unsupported conjecture.
notes.notDisconfirmed: It is disconfirmed by accepted beliefs about Jewish burial procedures for executed criminals. Those practices make the supposed temporary placement in Joseph's own tomb and later transfer to a criminals' graveyard historically unlikely.
notes.exceeds: No historian thinks this hypothesis outstrips its rivals on the historical criteria. It is not a serious competitor to the resurrection hypothesis.

scope.t: The displaced body hypothesis has narrow explanatory scope.

power.t: Klasner's hypothesis obviously has no explanatory power with regard to the appearances and the origin of the disciples' belief in the resurrection since it says nothing about these.

plausibility.t: The hypothesis is implausible for a number of reasons.

adHoc.t: The theory is not greatly ad hoc, but it is somewhat ad hoc.

notDisconfirmed.t: The theory is disconfirmed from what we know about Jewish burial procedures for criminals such as I just mentioned a moment ago.

exceeds.t: Again, no historian thinks that this hypothesis outstrips its rivals in meeting these criteria.

scoreNotes.summary: Raised scope to moderate because Craig explicitly grants the apparent-death hypothesis adequate scope, while keeping power, plausibility, ad hocness, and disconfirmation very low.
scoreNotes.scope: Raised to 0.58 because Craig says the apparent-death hypothesis does have adequate explanatory scope in the bare sense that it can be framed to address the three main facts.
scoreNotes.plausibility: Lowered slightly to 0.10 to reflect near-impossibility given Roman execution practices.
scoreNotes.notDisconfirmed: Very low: conflicts strongly with medical/historical evidence.

notes.scope: The apparent-death hypothesis has adequate scope in a bare sense because it can be framed to cover the empty tomb, the appearances, and the disciples' mistaken belief by claiming Jesus survived, escaped, and was then taken to be risen. The issue is not formal coverage but whether that story actually explains the evidence well.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is poor. Conspiratorial versions collapse back into the conspiracy theory, while non-conspiratorial versions cannot explain how Jesus would leave a sealed tomb or why a gravely wounded survivor would produce belief in a glorified conqueror of death rather than belief that he had simply not died.
notes.plausibility: The theory fails miserably on plausibility. Roman executioners were reliable, Jesus' side was pierced, and the extent of the scourging, crucifixion, and entombment makes survival virtually physically impossible.
notes.adHoc: The theory becomes highly ad hoc, especially in its conspiratorial forms. Some versions require speculative potions, secret societies, and hidden alliances, all without independent evidence.
notes.notDisconfirmed: It is massively disconfirmed by accepted medical facts about what scourging and crucifixion do to a body, and by the evidence that Jesus did not simply continue living among his disciples after the crucifixion.
notes.exceeds: It does not outperform rival explanations on the historical criteria. It is a badly failing hypothesis that has been virtually abandoned by New Testament historians.

scope.t: The apparent death hypothesis does have adequate explanatory scope.

power.t: The appearance of a half-dead man desperately in need of bandaging and medical attention would hardly have elicited in the disciples the belief that he was the risen Lord and the conqueror of death!

plausibility.t: Roman executioners could be relied upon to see that their victims were dead.

adHoc.t: The apparent death hypothesis, especially in its conspiratorial versions, can become enormously ad hoc.

notDisconfirmed.t: The apparent death hypothesis is massively disconfirmed by medical facts concerning what would happen to a person who had been scourged and then crucified.

exceeds.t: This theory has virtually no defenders today among New Testament historians.

scoreNotes.summary: Lowered to reflect Craig's argument that early dating and multiple attestation make wholesale legendary development unlikely.
scoreNotes.adHoc: Remains moderately high (0.45) because legend theories typically must be conjoined with others (e.g., hallucination).

notes.scope: The legend hypothesis is not a self-contained explanation of the three facts. In Strauss's version it effectively denies the empty tomb, borrows hallucination to handle the appearances, and appeals to myth and legend chiefly to explain the disciples' belief, so the theory's scope as a unified explanation is fragmented.
notes.power: Its explanatory power is especially weak on the origin of the disciples' belief. There was no Christianity yet to generate resurrection belief, pagan myths were neither genuine parallels nor causal influences, and Jewish beliefs would have pointed the disciples toward the end-time resurrection of all the dead or perhaps revivification, not Jesus' isolated resurrection to glory.
notes.plausibility: The theory is historically implausible. Jesus and his followers were first-century Judean Jews, not participants in pagan dying-and-rising cults, and the supposed pagan parallels have largely collapsed under closer scrutiny.
notes.adHoc: To make the hypothesis work, one must deny the empty tomb, supplement the appearances with another theory, and then posit one of several unsupported sources for the disciples' belief, whether Christian, pagan, or Jewish. Because each option fails on its own terms, the explanation survives only by layering conjectures.
notes.notDisconfirmed: The theory conflicts with beliefs often treated as historically established: the empty tomb is widely recognized as historical, resurrection faith is primitive rather than a late development, pagan parallels are spurious, and Second Temple Judaism did not expect an isolated resurrection of the Messiah within history.
notes.exceeds: Legend remains a live modern option rather than a defunct one, but if resurrection is excluded, the birth of the Christian movement remains unexplained. On this analysis, the legend hypothesis does not emerge as a superior rival.

scope.t: Strauss' hypothesis is, in effect, a denial of the fact of the empty tomb.

power.t: Left to themselves, the disciples would not have come to believe that Jesus was risen from the dead.

plausibility.t: The parallels turned out to be spurious.

adHoc.t: even the most skeptical critic has to posit some mysterious X to get the movement going.
adHoc.src: Defenders 3: Doctrine of Christ (Part 43) (quoting R. H. Fuller)

notDisconfirmed.t: The belief that Jesus was risen from the dead is the primitive belief of the earliest Christian disciples.

exceeds.t: The birth and rapid rise of the Christian Church . . . remain an unsolved enigma
